explanation: The natural language describes that the property involving prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 holds globally (for all time) and for every DM_i (which, in LTL, is typically represented by universally quantifying over the index, but with atomic propositions given and no indices, we interpret this as universally for all occurrences). Since the natural language doesn't specify a temporal relationship among the properties, the simplest correct global assertion is that (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2) hold at all times.

The statement says that certain properties (prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2) are true for every DM_i. Since LTL formulas must be written over atomic propositions, and no temporal constraints or additional context are provided, the natural translation is that globally (at all times), all three propositions hold.

The property states that for every DM_i, prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 are in some way related to holding globally. Since the text isn't fully clear about the precise relationship, the most general and direct interpretation is that all three propositions hold globally at all times. Therefore, we use the globally operator with their conjunction.

The requirement describes that for every DM_i (i.e., universally, at all points), Local Node must satisfy prop_0. Also, prop_1 and prop_2, thus (implies) prop_3. Since the whole property holds for every DM_i, this suggests a 'globally' (G) operator. The structure is: if prop_0 holds, and also prop_1, and also prop_2, then prop_3 must hold at that point. Alternatively, for all times, if (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2) is true, then prop_3 must be true. This is formalized as G( (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_3 ).

The statement implies that always, at any Local Node (represented by DM_i), if prop_0 holds, and prop_1 holds, and prop_2 holds, then prop_3 must also hold. Since this should hold for every node and at all times, we use the Global (G) operator with the implication.

The sentence describes that for every DM_i, the Local Node must satisfy prop_0, which also means prop_1 and prop_2 must hold, thus prop_3 must hold as a consequence. It implies that globally, whenever prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 all hold, then prop_3 must also hold. This is expressed by the LTL formula: G((prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_3).

The sentence states that always, if prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 hold, then prop_3 must also hold. Since this property holds for every DM_i, this is a universal (global) property. We encode this with the "globally" operator (G), capturing that at all times, if prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 hold, then prop_3 must also hold.

The sentence says that always, if at any point prop_0 holds, and prop_1 and prop_2 also hold, then prop_3 must hold as a result. This is a universal requirement (for every DM_i), so we use the 'globally' (G) operator and express the implication.

The property states that for all DM_i, some property holds involving prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2. Since the exact temporal relationship is not specified, but it says 'as shown...'—implying they are important components—we conservatively assume all must always hold. The global (G) operator expresses that these propositions always hold.

The statement seems to indicate that a property involving prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 must hold universally. Since there is no explicit temporal relation given, the standard interpretation is that all three propositions must hold globally (at all times).
